subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
solution:
Let the masses of the three parts be m', m' and 2m'.

Momentum of first part p1=m'×30=30m' and momentum of second part p2=m'×40=40m'.

Third part will fly in a direction opposite to the direction of resultant of p1 and p2, so that total momentum of the system after explosion remains zero.

Momentum of the third part is p3=p12+p22=30m'2+40m'2=50m'

Velocity of third part, v=50m'2m'=25 m s-1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
solution:
Let initial velocity of bullet be u.

Given, mass of bullet m=10 g and mass of ball M=200 g,

Initially ball is at height 5 m and at rest, the only acceleration acting is due to gravity.

Time of flight of each ball and bullet is t=2hg.

Now, velocity of ball and bullet as v1=302hg and v2=1202hg

As the collision is elastic, so applying conservation of linear momentum.

mu+M0=Mv1+mv2

⇒0.01u=0.230g2h+0.01120g2h

⇒u=300+60=360 m s-1

$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kx}^2=\frac{1}{2}(10)(3)^2-\left[\frac{1}{2}(15)(2)^2\right]$
$\Rightarrow 90-60=30=3000 x^2$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}^2 \Rightarrow \frac{30}{3000}=\frac{1}{100} \\ & \mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{10} \mathrm{~m}.\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
solution:
Initial position of centre of mass: xCM=100+30×x40=3x4

Final position of the centre of mass: x'CM=106+30×x-y40=6+3x-y4

As position of centre of mass remains same,

xCM=x'CM ⇒3x4=6+3x-y4⇒3x=6+3x-3y  ⇒y=2 cm

Alternate method, ∆xCOM=m1∆x1+m2∆x2m1+m2⇒0=10×6+30y⇒y=-2 cm

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
solution:
As we know, impulse is the area under the curve of the force vs. time graph. So, analysing each figure area under curve.

a A1=12×1.0×0.5=14 N‐s

b A2=0.5×2=1 N‐s

c A3=12×1×0.75=38 N‐s

d A4=12×2×0.5=12 N‐s

From above calculations, area under curve in figure (b) is maximum. Thus, fig (b) impulse is highest.

options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation of Statement-1.

solution: If it is a completely inelastic collision then $$ \begin{gathered} m_1 v_1+m_2 v_2=m_1 v+m_2 v \\ v=\frac{m_1 v_1+m_2 v_2}{m_1+m_2} \\ \mathrm{~K} \cdot \mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{p}_1^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}_1}+\frac{\mathrm{p}_2^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}_2} \end{gathered} $$ as $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}_1$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}_2}$ both simultaneously cannot be zero therefore total KE cannot be lost.

solution:
The law of conservation of momentum is given as

m1u+m2u'=m1+m2v'

The given data is 

u=vu'=0 m s-1m1=mm2=2m

Using the conservation of momentum equation,

mv+2m(0)=(m+2m)v'⇒v'=mv3m=v3

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution:
Statement I: Total kinetic energy of a system equals $\sum_i \frac{1}{2}m_i v_i^2$. This is TRUE by definition.
Statement II: Total KE can be decomposed as $KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2}M v_{cm}^2 + \sum_i \frac{1}{2}m_i v_i'^2$, where the first term is kinetic energy of center of mass motion and the second is kinetic energy of particles relative to the center of mass. This is TRUE by the energy decomposition theorem (Koenig's theorem).
Both statements are true.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
solution:
$\mathrm{KE}=\frac{\mathrm{p}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$
Same momentum, so less mass means more KE. So $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{2}$ will have max. $\mathrm{KE}$.
